title: Twenty-eight regulars
summary: A French waiter starts to teach a new colleague the Friday-evening regulars: who eats what, who never says thank you, and whose bread is never put on the table.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: Léa. Come. We have twenty minutes before the doors open.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: Today is Friday. Twenty-eight regulars between six and nine.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: You will write notes. In two months, you will know them. In a year, you will not need notes.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: One. Mr Lefèvre. Six fifteen. Table two. The lamb. A glass of red wine — he does not finish it. Water without asking. He never says thank you.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: He pays. He smiles a small smile. That is his thank you.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: Two. Madame Boucher. Six twenty. Seventy-eight. Brings her dog. Soup, fish, no wine. She loves bread, but her doctor said no bread after a stroke in 2019. So we do not put bread on her table. It is easier than her saying no every Friday.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: Three. The Bernards. Father and teenage son. Six thirty. They always argue. They order the same thing — steak. The father pays. The son does not say goodbye. We do not stand near their table.

body.speaker: Mr Girard
body.text: Four years. We hope this Friday is different.

annotations.meaningVi: không cần phải hỏi / tự động (không đợi được yêu cầu)
mỉm cười nhẹ (cấu trúc lặp danh từ-động từ cùng gốc — cognitive object)
con trai tuổi thiếu niên (13–19 tuổi)
gọi cùng một món (collocation: order + thing/dish)
chào tạm biệt (cụm từ cố định)
không đứng gần (cụm chỉ hành vi tránh né để giữ bình yên)
dễ hơn là ... (cấu trúc so sánh: easier than + V-ing)
không uống/ăn hết (không hoàn thành — dùng cho đồ ăn, đồ uống)

grammarNotes.title: Adverbs of frequency: 'always', 'never', 'every Friday'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để chỉ tần suất, A2 dùng các trạng từ như 'always' (luôn) và 'never' (không bao giờ), đứng trước động từ thường. Trong bài: 'He never says thank you.' 'They always argue.' 'Every Friday' (hàng tuần thứ Sáu) đứng đầu hoặc cuối câu. So sánh: 'always' rất cao, 'never' rất thấp; ở giữa có 'often', 'sometimes'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Frequency adverbs like 'always' (highest) and 'never' (lowest) go before main verbs. The dialogue: 'He never says thank you.' 'They always argue.' 'Every Friday' (a habitual time frame) goes at the start or end of a sentence. Between always and never sit 'often' and 'sometimes'.

translationVi:
Ông Girard: Léa. Lại đây. Còn hai mươi phút trước khi mở cửa.
Léa: Vâng, ông Girard.
Ông Girard: Hôm nay là thứ Sáu. Hai mươi tám khách quen trong khoảng từ sáu đến chín.
Léa: Tôi không nhớ nổi hai mươi tám người.
Ông Girard: Hôm nay em viết ghi chú. Hai tháng nữa em sẽ biết họ. Một năm nữa em không cần ghi chú.
Léa: Kể em ba người đầu.
Ông Girard: Một. Ông Lefèvre. Sáu giờ mười lăm. Bàn hai. Thịt cừu. Một ly vang đỏ — không uống hết. Mang nước không cần hỏi. Ông không bao giờ nói cảm ơn.
Léa: Không bao giờ nói cảm ơn?
Ông Girard: Ông ấy trả tiền. Mỉm cười nhỏ. Đó là lời cảm ơn của ông.
Léa: Vâng.
Ông Girard: Hai. Bà Boucher. Sáu giờ hai mươi. Bảy mươi tám tuổi. Mang theo chó. Súp, cá, không rượu. Bà rất thích bánh mì, nhưng bác sĩ dặn không ăn bánh mì sau cơn đột quỵ năm 2019. Nên chúng ta không đặt bánh mì lên bàn bà. Dễ hơn việc tuần nào bà cũng phải từ chối.
Léa: Em hiểu.
Ông Girard: Ba. Cha con nhà Bernard. Cha và con trai tuổi teen. Sáu giờ ba mươi. Họ luôn cãi nhau. Họ gọi cùng một món — bít tết. Người cha trả tiền. Người con không chào tạm biệt. Mình không đứng gần bàn họ.
Léa: Họ cãi nhau bao lâu rồi?
Ông Girard: Bốn năm. Mình hi vọng thứ Sáu này khác.

questions.prompt: How many Friday regulars are there, and at what hours?
What does Mr Girard say will happen by the end of one year?
options: Léa will not need notes anymore.
questions.explanation: 'In a year, you will not need notes.'

options: A regular who comes at 6:15 to table two, orders lamb, takes a glass of red wine but does not finish it, and never says thank you.

questions.prompt: How is Mr Lefèvre's 'thank you' expressed?
options: He pays and smiles a small smile.

questions.prompt: Why don't they put bread on Madame Boucher's table?
options: Her doctor said no bread after a stroke in 2019; it is easier than her saying no every Friday.

questions.prompt: What does Madame Boucher bring with her?

options: A father and teenage son who always argue and order the same steak.

questions.prompt: What do the waiters do around the Bernards' table?
options: They do not stand near it.
questions.explanation: 'We do not stand near their table.'

questions.prompt: How long have the Bernards argued, and what do the waiters hope?
options: Four years; they hope this Friday is different.
Four months; they hope they leave.
Forty years; they hope they pay more.
One year; they hope they invite the staff.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Long-term restaurant service is not memorising orders — it is quietly arranging the room around each person's specific habits and history.
Bread should always be on the table.
questions.explanation: Each regular has a tailored handling rule.
questions.hint: Notice the small adaptations Mr Girard names.

cloze.template:
Mr Girard: Léa. Come. We have twenty minutes before the doors {{b1}}.
Léa: Yes, Mr Girard.
Mr Girard: Today is Friday. {{b2}} regulars between six and nine.
Léa: I cannot remember twenty-eight people.
Mr Girard: You will write {{b3}}. In two months, you will know them. In a year, you will not need notes.
Léa: Tell me the first three.
Mr Girard: One. Mr Lefèvre. Six fifteen. Table two. The {{b4}}. A glass of red wine — he does not finish it. Water without asking. He {{b5}} says thank you.
Léa: He never says thank you?
Mr Girard: He pays. He smiles a small smile. That is his thank you.
Léa: Okay.
Mr Girard: Two. Madame Boucher. Six twenty. Seventy-eight. Brings her {{b6}}. Soup, fish, no wine. She loves bread, but her doctor said no bread after a {{b7}} in 2019. So we do not put bread on her table. It is easier than her saying no every Friday.
Léa: I understand.
Mr Girard: Three. The Bernards. Father and teenage son. Six thirty. They {{b8}} argue. They order the same thing — {{b9}}. The father pays. The son does not say goodbye. We do not stand near their table.
Léa: How long have they argued?
Mr Girard: Four years. We hope this Friday is {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: They hope this Friday is different.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Girard's training is not about memorising orders but about small adaptations: no bread for Madame Boucher, no waiter near the Bernards. Is there a person you serve regularly — a family member, a customer, a student — where the kindest thing you do is a small adjustment they never have to ask for? What is it?
